title: Adventures In Baby Getting

jokes.text: Oh, man, not another election. Why do we have to choose our leaders? Isn't that what we have the Supreme Court for?
jokes.setup: Homer complaining about having to vote in elections
jokes.punchline: Suggesting the Supreme Court should choose leaders instead of voters
jokes.explanation: Strong opening joke that captures Homer's ignorance about civics while making a pointed reference to Bush v. Gore. Good character integration and topical bite.

jokes.text: But I'm a 40-year-old white guy who didn't go to college and gets all his news from monitors at gas stations. In you go.
jokes.setup: Homer protesting voter ID requirements
jokes.punchline: His demographic description perfectly matches the target voter profile, so he's immediately let through
jokes.explanation: Excellent satire of voter suppression with perfect character specificity. Homer's self-description is both accurate and damning, and the immediate reversal is well-crafted.

jokes.text: I already got one wife telling me to eat healthy. Plus he promised me death panels, and Grandpa's still alive.
jokes.setup: Homer considering Barack Obama as a candidate
jokes.punchline: Complaining that Obama's 'death panels' haven't killed Grandpa yet
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that skewers both political misinformation and Homer's callousness toward family. The casual acceptance of death panels as real is perfectly Homer.

jokes.text: I expect the leader of the free world to go commando. Plus his horse totally choked at the Olympics.
jokes.setup: Homer's reasons for rejecting Mitt Romney
jokes.punchline: Bizarre underwear requirement and blaming Romney for his Olympic horse's performance
jokes.explanation: Absurd logic that's funny but feels more scattered than focused. The underwear and horse complaints don't build on each other effectively.

jokes.text: On the other hand, he did invent Obamacare.
jokes.setup: Homer listing Romney's negatives
jokes.punchline: Crediting Romney with creating Obamacare (referencing Romneycare)
jokes.explanation: Smart political reference but requires specific knowledge to land fully. Good economy but less universally accessible.

jokes.text: Medical deduction for personality implant? He's got six wives all named Ann. The government paid him taxes for five years!
jokes.setup: Homer reading Romney's tax returns
jokes.punchline: Increasingly ridiculous tax return revelations
jokes.explanation: Good escalation structure with absurdist elements. Each revelation gets more ridiculous, building comedic momentum effectively.

jokes.text: You are now being outsourced.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to tell the press about the tax returns
jokes.punchline: The voting machine casually announces his outsourcing
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy and unexpected dark turn. The matter-of-fact delivery makes the absurdist premise land harder.

jokes.text: Oh, I hate being sucked into tubes!
jokes.setup: Homer being transported via pneumatic tube
jokes.punchline: His casual complaint suggests this happens to him regularly
jokes.explanation: Mild callback humor that works but isn't particularly memorable. The familiarity helps but doesn't elevate it much.

jokes.text: Well, at least I got a steady job. D'oh!
jokes.setup: Homer trying to find a silver lining in being outsourced
jokes.punchline: Immediately realizing he doesn't actually have a job, followed by his signature 'D'oh!'
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer moment with good timing. The 'D'oh!' is earned through his realization, making it more than just a catchphrase drop.

jokes.text: Hummingbirds, hum and get it!
jokes.setup: Nature documentary style narration
jokes.punchline: Terrible pun on 'hum' that sounds like a bad comedian's setup
jokes.explanation: Weak pun that feels forced and doesn't serve much purpose. It's the kind of throwaway line that fills time but doesn't add much.

jokes.text: Homer, when are you going to fix this faucet? I'm on it! Are you going to fix the faucet or not? For the third time this year, yes!
jokes.setup: Marge repeatedly asking about the faucet
jokes.punchline: Homer's claim that 'For the third time this year' counts as urgency
jokes.explanation: Good character-driven humor that captures the universal domestic dynamic. Homer's defensive pride in his procrastination is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: When we first got married, he'd only take six months to do things.
jokes.setup: Marge reflecting on Homer's procrastination getting worse
jokes.punchline: Six months was considered fast for Homer
jokes.explanation: Excellent deadpan delivery that reveals the depth of Homer's incompetence. The nostalgic framing makes it even funnier.

jokes.text: Mom, how come you didn't do my homework last night?
jokes.setup: Bart addressing his mother casually
jokes.punchline: Expecting his mom to do his homework as a normal responsibility
jokes.explanation: Good character moment for Bart's entitled laziness, though not groundbreaking. The casualness sells it well.

jokes.text: Oh, man, I got to climb stairs? Forget it. No one stays in the sinkhole!
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy encountering stairs while trying to escape a sinkhole
jokes.punchline: Choosing to stay in the dangerous sinkhole rather than climb stairs
jokes.explanation: Perfect Comic Book Guy moment that captures his laziness and dramatic declarations. The absurd choice is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: While this sinkhole is larger than the Sarlacc pit which swallowed Boba Fett, it is smaller than the Geonosis battlerena where Jango Fett was beheaded by Mace Windu. Weren't those two places about the same size? I didn't know this was an ambush!
jokes.setup: News reporter using Star Wars references to describe sinkhole size
jokes.punchline: Another reporter questioning the comparison, causing the first to panic
jokes.explanation: Clever meta-joke about the arbitrariness of pop culture size comparisons in news. The panic response adds a nice layer.

jokes.text: Eh, you know, in this town, you learn to adjust to things: runaway monorails, NASCAR star Jeff Gordon.
jokes.setup: Moe explaining Springfield's resilience
jokes.punchline: Equating a NASCAR driver's presence with actual disasters
jokes.explanation: Good callback to classic episode while adding new absurd element. Moe's matter-of-fact delivery works well.

jokes.text: That plank's only for coming in!
jokes.setup: Someone trying to leave Moe's bar via a plank over the sinkhole
jokes.punchline: Moe's arbitrary one-way plank rule
jokes.explanation: Classic Moe pettiness with perfect economy. The arbitrary rule-making is quintessentially his character.

jokes.text: Try to land on the other traitors.
jokes.setup: Someone falling into the sinkhole
jokes.punchline: Moe's helpful suggestion to cushion the fall with other people, calling them traitors
jokes.explanation: Dark humor with great character voice. Moe's casual cruelty and paranoia about 'traitors' is perfectly him.

jokes.text: But my car's down there!
jokes.setup: Mayor announcing they'll fill the sinkhole with the tire fire
jokes.punchline: Someone's mundane concern about their car amid the chaos
jokes.explanation: Serviceable background joke that provides grounded concern amid absurdity. Not particularly memorable but functional.

jokes.text: For the first time, my administration has solved a problem.
jokes.setup: The sinkhole being filled successfully
jokes.punchline: Mayor admitting this is his first actual accomplishment
jokes.explanation: Good political satire with appropriate character voice. The admission of incompetence is both funny and believable.

jokes.text: So, just when my dad finishes his online degree in Sinkhole Engineering, they go and fill it in!
jokes.setup: Lisa commenting on the sinkhole being filled
jokes.punchline: Homer apparently got a degree in the extremely specific field of sinkhole engineering
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist concept with good timing. The specificity of 'sinkhole engineering' and Homer's terrible timing is very funny.

jokes.text: Are the credits transferable? No. That guy has a bone to pick with the Board of Regents.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Homer's sinkhole engineering degree
jokes.punchline: The fake degree isn't transferable and Homer apparently has beef with academic administration
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up that extends the absurdist premise. The bureaucratic details make Homer's fake education more believable and funnier.

jokes.text: I don't know. Dork farm? Who cares?
jokes.setup: Discussing where Lisa is going
jokes.punchline: Bart's casual dismissal with 'dork farm'
jokes.explanation: Classic Bart dismissiveness with good economy. 'Dork farm' is creative enough to be memorable.

jokes.text: I'm going to a accident!
jokes.setup: Ralph trying to follow the other kids
jokes.punchline: His grammatically incorrect declaration of going to 'a accident'
jokes.explanation: Standard Ralph malapropism that's funny but not exceptional. The character integration is perfect but the concept is familiar.

jokes.text: Does she even know how much to tip?
jokes.setup: Seeing Lisa take a taxi
jokes.punchline: Bart's concern is about tipping etiquette rather than her safety
jokes.explanation: Good subversion of expected concern. Instead of worrying about Lisa's safety, Bart focuses on mundane social protocol.

jokes.text: The five boxing wizards jump quickly.
jokes.setup: Mystery about what Lisa is writing
jokes.punchline: It's a pangram - a sentence containing every letter of the alphabet
jokes.explanation: Clever setup for the cursive writing revelation. Works well for viewers who recognize pangrams and foreshadows the plot nicely.

jokes.text: My sister's finally become... mildly interesting.
jokes.setup: Bart observing Lisa's mysterious behavior
jokes.punchline: The extremely mild praise of 'mildly interesting'
jokes.explanation: Great deadpan delivery showing Bart's grudging admission that Lisa might not be completely boring.

jokes.text: Ah, they never stop for blue-heads.
jokes.setup: Marge trying to hail a taxi
jokes.punchline: Her blue beehive hair makes taxi discrimination seem logical
jokes.explanation: Great visual-based joke that acknowledges Marge's unusual appearance. The matter-of-fact delivery makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Well, I think the Tissan Sensibla is the car for you. It's got all the features that a modern woman wants, including all manner of makeup mirrors.
jokes.setup: Car salesman talking to Marge
jokes.punchline: Patronizing assumption about what women want in cars, with fake car name
jokes.explanation: Good satire of sexist car sales tactics. The fake car name adds to the sleazy salesman character.

jokes.text: This baby comes with an RX-12 2-cylinder.
jokes.setup: Marge asking about engine power
jokes.punchline: Salesman gives impressive-sounding but actually pathetic engine specs
jokes.explanation: Solid joke about deceptive sales tactics. The technical-sounding nonsense covers up the terrible specs well.

jokes.text: It says on your Facebook page you have no kids. I have two Yorkies, and I'll be talking about you to them tonight!
jokes.setup: Marge catching the salesman in lies
jokes.punchline: His pathetic threat to complain about her to his dogs
jokes.explanation: Great escalation of the salesman's desperation. The image of him complaining to his Yorkshire terriers is perfectly pathetic.

jokes.text: Room for two adults, three kids and zero grandpas.
jokes.setup: Homer praising the car's capacity
jokes.punchline: Specifically excluding Grandpa from the family count
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that perfectly captures Homer's casual cruelty toward his father. The specificity of 'zero grandpas' makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Let Automobile Von Bismarck give it a try.
jokes.setup: Car won't start for Homer
jokes.punchline: Barney's ridiculous self-appointed automotive expertise title
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment for Barney. The grandiose Germanic title for basic car knowledge is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: Worst car ever! I can't believe it!
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy encountering car problems
jokes.punchline: Using his signature 'worst ever' catchphrase about cars instead of comics
jokes.explanation: Solid character callback applying his usual hyperbole to a new situation.

jokes.text: The wipers wipe backwards, and the defroster lines on the back window are too close together!
jokes.setup: Marge listing car complaints
jokes.punchline: Increasingly petty and specific complaints about minor details
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Marge's growing irrationality. The specificity of the complaints makes her seem more neurotic.

jokes.text: As a mechanic, I'm not totally qualified to judge mental health... So, you're not totally unqualified.
jokes.setup: Mechanic disclaiming his psychological expertise
jokes.punchline: Homer's logical but unhelpful interpretation of the double negative
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that shows Homer's occasional logical moments. The mechanic's attempt at professional caution backfires nicely.

jokes.text: Wow, an auto mechanic who tells the truth.
jokes.setup: Mechanic suggesting Marge has psychological issues
jokes.punchline: Homer's appreciation for brutal honesty from a typically dishonest profession
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor about mechanic stereotypes. Homer's misplaced priorities are in character.

jokes.text: She's an '83 Yugo. I don't do laundry or kiss boo-boos!
jokes.setup: Mechanic offering a loaner car
jokes.punchline: The car itself speaks, setting boundaries like a babysitter
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist concept - a talking car that's also terrible and sets limits. The Yugo reference adds to the patheticness.

jokes.text: With this car, we're basically saying no more babies.
jokes.setup: Marge talking about the backseat being tight
jokes.punchline: Connecting car size to family planning decisions
jokes.explanation: Excellent setup for the episode's main plot. The logical leap from car space to reproduction is perfectly Marge-like.

jokes.text: With this car, we're basically saying no more babies.
jokes.setup: Marge analyzing the car's tight space
jokes.punchline: Connecting car size to family planning decisions
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the episode's main plot, showing how practical decisions affect family dynamics.

jokes.text: An 'on purpose' baby?!
jokes.setup: Marge saying she wants another baby
jokes.punchline: Homer's horrified clarification that she means a planned pregnancy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer reaction that implies all their children were accidents. The horror in his voice at the concept of planning is excellent.

jokes.text: Oh, I really wish I had fixed that faucet.
jokes.setup: Marge wanting a baby
jokes.punchline: Homer connecting this crisis to his procrastination about home repairs
jokes.explanation: Good callback that shows Homer's tendency to blame everything on his procrastination. The connection is illogical but in character.

jokes.text: Her soft spot's been skulled over for the last three months!
jokes.setup: Homer arguing Maggie isn't a baby
jokes.punchline: Using medical terminology to argue against Maggie's baby status
jokes.explanation: Good use of technical knowledge for comic effect. Homer's desperate attempt to disqualify Maggie as a baby is funny.

jokes.text: When your wife looks at you full of hope, with an idea that would destroy life as we know it... just smile, nod and stall, stall, stall until she changes her mind.
jokes.setup: Homer's internal monologue about handling Marge
jokes.punchline: His strategy of deception and delay
jokes.explanation: Good insight into Homer's cowardly relationship strategy. The dramatic phrasing makes his fear seem overblown and funny.

jokes.text: All right! 'Try it' means sex.
jokes.setup: Marge saying they'll 'try it' tonight
jokes.punchline: Homer's excited clarification of euphemistic language
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer moment of stating the obvious with enthusiasm. The character integration is perfect but the joke is straightforward.

jokes.text: Just appreciating... how lucky... I am to be... with... you.
jokes.setup: Marge questioning his pause
jokes.punchline: Homer's labored, obviously fake romantic response with continued pausing
jokes.explanation: Good use of timing and pauses for comedy. Homer's obvious stalling while trying to seem romantic is well-executed.

jokes.text: Maybe I should go on antipsychotic meds.
jokes.setup: Bart seeing something confusing about Lisa
jokes.punchline: His casual consideration of psychiatric medication
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that's funny because it's Bart casually considering heavy medication. The matter-of-fact delivery works well.

jokes.text: I'd hate for your carpet to fade.
jokes.setup: Bart in Lisa's room doing something suspicious
jokes.punchline: His fake considerate excuse for lowering her blinds
jokes.explanation: Good Bart misdirection with fake domesticity. The overly considerate excuse is transparently false but delivered well.

jokes.text: Today's was 'mousetraps are not slippers' or something.
jokes.setup: Bart saying he writes on the chalkboard
jokes.punchline: The bizarre and unhelpful nature of his punishment sentences
jokes.explanation: Funny absurdist take on Bart's chalkboard punishments. The specific randomness of the sentence is good, and his casual dismissal adds to it.

jokes.text: A gentleman doesn't ask, and a lady doesn't tell.
jokes.setup: Bart asking what Lisa's up to
jokes.punchline: Lisa's prim deflection using formal language
jokes.explanation: Good character moment for Lisa's tendency toward formal speech. The phrase works well for deflection while staying in character.

jokes.text: And let me tell you, a towel rack will not support your weight.
jokes.setup: Marge discussing her and Homer's attempts at conception
jokes.punchline: TMI about failed sexual positions involving bathroom fixtures
jokes.explanation: Good cringe humor with Marge oversharing about her sex life. The specific detail about the towel rack makes it funnier and more uncomfortable.

jokes.text: X's for eyes.
jokes.setup: Doctor showing Homer's sperm test results
jokes.punchline: Cartoonish dead sperm imagery described matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy in describing dead sperm as cartoon characters. The visual is immediately clear and funny.

jokes.text: X's for eyes.
jokes.setup: Marge looking at Homer's sperm sample results
jokes.punchline: Describing dead sperm with cartoon death symbolism
jokes.explanation: Excellent visual metaphor using cartoon death imagery for medical results.

jokes.text: That's why I wrap my plums in tinfoil every day.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Homer's sterility
jokes.punchline: Moe's crude protective measure for his testicles
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moe moment with his crude solution and euphemistic language. The daily routine aspect makes it funnier.

jokes.text: That's how I got the money to buy you your necklace.
jokes.setup: Homer admitting to selling sperm
jokes.punchline: Connecting romantic gesture to biological commerce
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor that recontextualizes romantic gestures. The connection between love and bodily fluids is perfectly Homer-esque.

jokes.text: I think that Shiva used to be Paul Bunyan.
jokes.setup: Homer looking at decorations
jokes.punchline: His theory about statue recycling between different mythologies
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist observation that actually makes weird sense. Homer's casual cultural confusion is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Outmoded, no room in the budget, and all the teachers forgot how to make a capital 'Q.' Two loops and a flourish.
jokes.setup: Explaining why cursive isn't taught
jokes.punchline: Teachers' incompetence with basic letter formation, followed by Lisa's easy solution
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from budget to incompetence. Lisa's simple solution highlights the absurdity of the teachers' ignorance.

jokes.text: I'll take it from here, wingman.
jokes.setup: Homer approaching the bed
jokes.punchline: The bed dismissing Homer like a pickup artist's assistant
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist concept of furniture as a suave seducer. The casual dismissal of Homer is perfectly executed.

jokes.text: I guess it started sometime before Bart was born.
jokes.setup: Marge asking how long Homer hasn't wanted another baby
jokes.punchline: Admitting he didn't want Bart before Bart even existed
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about not wanting children that's both shocking and perfectly in character for Homer.

jokes.text: Room for one, please.
jokes.setup: Marge angry at Homer
jokes.punchline: Her matter-of-fact request for separate accommodation
jokes.explanation: Good understated reaction from Marge. The polite phrasing of her anger is very in character.

jokes.text: Just yell across the house like you do everything else.
jokes.setup: Homer saying he didn't know how to tell her
jokes.punchline: Marge pointing out his usual communication style
jokes.explanation: Good marital humor about Homer's typical communication failures.

jokes.text: You were pocket-dialed!
jokes.setup: Homer's phone calling Marge
jokes.punchline: His defensive explanation that his pocket made the romantic gesture
jokes.explanation: Good use of modern technology for Homer's emotional avoidance. The idea that his pocket has better relationship instincts is funny.

jokes.text: Hey, the pocket wants what it wants.
jokes.setup: Homer explaining the pocket-dial
jokes.punchline: Anthropomorphizing his pocket's romantic desires
jokes.explanation: Great follow-up that extends the pocket anthropomorphization. The casual acceptance of pocket agency is absurdly funny.

jokes.text: The fat, pathetic slob I'd be with four kids.
jokes.setup: Homer seeing another father
jokes.punchline: His harsh but accurate assessment of his alternate self
jokes.explanation: Good self-aware humor from Homer. His brutal honesty about his own potential decline is both funny and sad.

jokes.text: Or it may be under the name Thad Supersperm.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to retrieve his sperm donation
jokes.punchline: His ridiculous pseudonym for sperm donation
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist name that perfectly captures Homer's grandiose but juvenile mindset. Very quotable and memorable.

jokes.text: The table with four legs is sturdier than the table with three. Cubes are made of cheese, but pyramids are schemes.
jokes.setup: Homer making a speech about wanting another child
jokes.punchline: His nonsensical metaphors that start logical but become completely absurd
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer speech that starts with reasonable metaphors then devolves into complete nonsense. The confidence in delivery makes it funnier.

jokes.text: I mean, there's always a little ketchup left in the bottle.
jokes.setup: Being told there's no more of his sperm sample
jokes.punchline: His crude food analogy for biological materials
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer metaphor that's both gross and logical. The comparison reduces biology to condiments in a very Homer way.

jokes.text: No, no, no, look in the mirror-- that's your argument.
jokes.setup: Homer asking to buy back his sperm sample
jokes.punchline: The man's brutal assessment of Homer's appearance as reason enough to decline
jokes.explanation: Sharp visual insult with good economy. The mirror reference makes it immediately clear and brutal.

jokes.text: I mean, I'm no Luis Guzman, but I'm all right.
jokes.setup: Homer defending his appearance
jokes.punchline: Using a character actor known for unconventional looks as his comparison point
jokes.explanation: Good celebrity reference that shows Homer's skewed self-perception. Using Guzman as a beauty standard is perfectly Homer.

jokes.text: remember that Corvette I had? I thought they only paid $50 a trip. Exactly.
jokes.setup: Marge asking how many samples Homer sold
jokes.punchline: The math revealing he made hundreds of donations to afford a Corvette
jokes.explanation: Excellent mathematical humor that lets the audience calculate Homer's prolific donation history. The economics are both horrifying and funny.

jokes.text: I don't want to bring more kids into a world full of my kids.
jokes.setup: Homer reconsidering having another child
jokes.punchline: His realization that his genetic proliferation might be a global threat
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor that connects Homer's donations to global overpopulation. The self-aware horror of his own genetics is perfectly crafted.

tmdb_overview: When Marge's car falls into a sink hole, she buys the perfect replacement to fit her family of five. But she quickly grows to hate it because, as she tells a shocked Homer, the car would be too small if she wanted to have another baby. Meanwhile, Bart and his friends are determined to find out what secret Lisa is keeping when Bart finds her cryptic messages and follows her sneaking off downtown for covert meetings after school.
